shell.footer: Deterministic scoring · Data stays in your browser

home.title: Separate a real problem from a clever mechanism.
home.lead: A short structured test shows hypothesis quality and suggests the next cheap step — without AI or false precision.
home.fine: This is an initial filter, not proof of viability.
home.card1Text: About the user, behavior, value, payment, and evidence.
home.card2Text: The result is calculated with transparent rules.

about.title: Briefly describe the idea
about.lead: A couple of concrete sentences is enough. This is not an investor pitch.

quiz.cheapTestTitle: What is the cheapest test you see for this idea right now?
quiz.cheapTestWhy: Does not affect the score, but becomes part of the final report and AI prompt.
quiz.cheapTestPlaceholder: For example: offer a manual service to ten small coffee shop owners…
quiz.strongestFactTitle: What specific fact is your strongest answer based on?
quiz.strongestFactWhy: Optional. Helps separate observation from assumption.
quiz.strongestFactPlaceholder: For example: three coffee shop owners complained about the same problem this week…
quiz.killCriterionTitle: What result would make you abandon the hypothesis or change it?
quiz.killCriterionWhy: Optional. A predefined criterion makes validation more honest.
quiz.killCriterionPlaceholder: For example: if none of 10 interviews are willing to pay more than the current solution…
quiz.frequencyTitle: How often does the problem occur?
quiz.frequencyWhy: Does not affect the score. For rare high-stakes scenarios, the cost of one case matters more than frequency.

history.title: Validation history
history.emptyText: Complete your first test — the result will be saved only in this browser.

result.disclaimer: The result does not prove the idea will succeed or fail. It shows the quality of the current hypothesis and helps choose the next step.
result.weaknessesEmpty: No obvious weak signals — but they still need to be checked.
result.stopText: They capped the final category even if the total score is higher.

ai.title: Ask AI
ai.lead: Open your chosen service — the prompt will already be in the input field. Just press Enter.
ai.copyFailed: Auto-copy unavailable. Copy the selected text manually
ai.editorHint: Edits apply only to the prompt copy and do not change test answers.
ai.warning: AI can offer an additional perspective, but its answer is not proof of demand. Validate conclusions through conversations, sign-ups, sales, and real user behavior.

beautifulMechanism.label: Beautiful mechanism
beautifulMechanism.description: The idea still has more interesting concept than confirmed problem. Do not move to development. First find a specific user and check whether they already try to solve this task.

weakHypothesis.label: Weak or incomplete hypothesis
weakHypothesis.description: The idea may have a useful feature or niche scenario, but business value is not yet clear enough. Clarify the audience, problem strength, and payment moment.

cheapTest.label: Worth running a cheap test
cheapTest.description: There are signs of a real problem, but not enough evidence yet. Do not build a full product. Run one short test and collect observable behavior.

strongHypothesis.label: Strong hypothesis to validate
strongHypothesis.description: The idea has a clear user, existing behavior, and noticeable value. The next stage is not full product development, but demand validation through sales, sign-ups, or manual service delivery.

recommendations.user: Describe one specific user and the situation in which the problem appears.
recommendations.behavior: Find 5 people and ask when they last faced this task and what they did.
recommendations.payment: Find an existing transaction or budget near which the product can earn money.
recommendations.alternatives: Ask users to walk through their current problem-solving process step by step.
recommendations.evidence: Do not stop at words. Look for effort, commitment, or payment.
recommendations.test: Formulate one key hypothesis and a way to test it manually without development.
recommendations.stakeholders: Clarify who actually uses the product, who decides to buy, and who pays.

prompt.intro: Analyze this business idea critically. Do not try to confirm its potential or invent advantages without grounds.
prompt.tasks: Find assumptions the user treats as facts. Show the main weak spots. Determine whether a real problem exists or only an interesting mechanism. Say who would pay for what specifically. Suggest one minimal test without full development. At the end, decide: validate now, refine the hypothesis, or archive.
prompt.format: Do not write a large business plan. The answer should be direct and compact.
prompt.name: Name

user.title: Can you name a specific person or type of business the product is for?
user.why: The more precise the user, the easier it is to validate the problem and find first interviewees.

options.text: No, the product is potentially for everyone
options.explanation: Audience is not defined yet.

options.text: There is a broad audience but no specific segment
options.explanation: There is direction, but the segment is still vague.

options.text: There is a specific user type with a clear situation
options.explanation: The user and their context are clear.

behavior.title: What do people already do to solve this problem?
behavior.why: Existing behavior is a stronger signal than stated interest.

options.text: Nothing yet — this is only an assumed need
options.explanation: No confirmed behavior.

options.text: Sometimes use workarounds
options.explanation: There are weak workaround scenarios.

options.text: Actively search for solutions, use services, spreadsheets, chats, or manual work
options.explanation: People already spend effort solving it.

pain.title: What does the user lose because of this problem?
pain.why: A strong loss creates motivation to act and pay. For rare scenarios — a deal, surgery, dispute — the cost of one case matters more than frequency.

options.text: Almost nothing — mostly inconvenience
options.explanation: The problem weakly affects outcomes.

options.text: Time, attention, or comfort
options.explanation: There are noticeable but moderate losses.

options.text: Money, customers, safety, an important opportunity, or a lot of work time
options.explanation: The cost of the problem is high.

urgency.title: How easily can the user postpone solving the problem?
urgency.why: Urgency shows whether a real moment of action will appear.

options.text: Can ignore it indefinitely
options.explanation: No moment forcing action.

options.text: Would be nice to solve
options.explanation: Solving helps, but is not required.

options.text: The problem must be solved at a specific moment
options.explanation: There is a clear moment of action.

stakeholders.title: Who uses the product, who makes the decision, and who pays?
stakeholders.why: In B2B and high-stakes scenarios, the user, decision maker, and payer are often different people.

options.text: Not thought through — seems like the same person
options.explanation: User, buyer, and payer roles are not separated.

options.text: General understanding, but roles may overlap
options.explanation: There is a picture, but role boundaries are still vague.

options.text: Clear who uses, who decides, and who pays
options.explanation: Roles are clear, even if they are different people.

payment.title: Is there a natural moment when the user is already ready to pay?
payment.why: A clear budget or transaction makes monetization easier to validate.

options.text: No — it is still unclear what they would pay for
options.explanation: Payment moment not found.

options.text: The user could theoretically buy a subscription or pay once
options.explanation: Payment is possible, but still hypothetical.

options.text: A transaction or dedicated budget already exists in this situation
options.explanation: There is an existing money flow.

alternatives.title: How well do current ways solve the problem?
alternatives.why: You need to be noticeably better than the existing process, not just newer.

options.text: Already good enough
options.explanation: Current alternatives are strong.

options.text: They work but require extra steps
options.explanation: There is moderate friction.

options.text: Expensive, unreliable, opaque, or require a lot of manual work
options.explanation: Alternatives have clear weaknesses.

access.title: Can you quickly find people to validate the idea?
access.why: Without access to users, even a good hypothesis is hard to test.

options.text: I do not know where to find such people
options.explanation: No channel to reach the audience.

options.text: I know a few communities or channels
options.explanation: There are potential channels.

options.text: I already talk to such people or can reach them directly
options.explanation: Users are accessible for validation.

evidence.title: What demand evidence do you already have?
evidence.why: Separate opinions, commitments, and money — each level is stronger than the last.

options.text: Words only: discussions, complaints, interest, compliments
options.explanation: There are opinions, but no action.

options.text: Observable effort or commitment: time, manual work, sign-up, pilot agreement
options.explanation: People are already doing something or committing.

options.text: Usage or payment: real use, pre-orders, transactions
options.explanation: There is behavior or money.

test.title: Can the key hypothesis be tested without full development?
test.why: This is not a measure of idea strength, but readiness for the next step. Not knowing how to test yet does not make the problem weaker.

options.text: I do not know how yet
options.explanation: The next experiment is not defined yet.

options.text: Can make a landing page, form, manual service, or interviews
options.explanation: There is a low-cost validation format.

options.text: There is a specific test that can launch in a few days
options.explanation: The next experiment is already clear.

frequency.title: How often does the problem occur?

options.text: Once or twice in a lifetime, or very rarely
Several times a year or in important situations
Monthly, weekly, or constantly
